def: A distressing inner restlessness with a compulsion to move, often mistaken for anxiety — a common early antipsychotic side effect.
Sudden, sustained muscle spasms (neck, jaw, eyes) hours to days after a dopamine blocker; a urgency treated with a parenteral anticholinergic.
An acute dystonic reaction in which the eyes forcibly roll upward and stay fixed there.
A dystonic spasm that twists and holds the neck to one side.
Lockjaw — painful clenching from spasm of the jaw muscles.
Spasm of the vocal cords that can obstruct the airway — a feared severe dystonic reaction.
Antipsychotic-induced tremor, rigidity, and slowed movement mimicking Parkinson disease; develops over weeks.
Slowness of movement — a cardinal parkinsonian sign.
Ratchet-like, jerky resistance felt when passively moving a limb — a parkinsonian sign.
A reduced, mask-like facial expression seen in parkinsonism.
Rhythmic shaking present at rest that eases with movement — typical of parkinsonism; a coarse tremor also flags lithium toxicity.
Late-onset, often irreversible involuntary movements (face, tongue, limbs) from chronic dopamine-blocker exposure.
Brief, irregular, dance-like involuntary movements.
Exaggerated deep-tendon reflexes — a key sign of serotonin toxicity.
Rhythmic involuntary muscle jerks on sudden stretch; leg-predominant clonus is a hallmark of serotonin toxicity.
Uniform, sustained increased muscle tone; 'lead-pipe' rigidity with fever suggests neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
A drop in blood pressure on standing that causes dizziness or fainting — common with alpha-1-blocking drugs.
An abnormally slow heart rate (under about 60 bpm).
Dilated pupils — part of the anticholinergic and sympathomimetic toxidromes.
Pinpoint pupils — a classic sign of opioid (and clonidine) toxicity.
Profuse sweating.
Dangerously raised body temperature — a feature of serotonin syndrome, NMS, and anticholinergic toxicity.
Unsteady, uncoordinated gait and movement — seen in lithium toxicity and Wernicke encephalopathy.
Weakness or paralysis of the eye muscles — part of the Wernicke encephalopathy triad.
A prolonged, painful erection unrelated to arousal — a urologic emergency linked to alpha-1 blockade (trazodone).
Inappropriate milk production from the breast, driven by elevated prolactin.
Absence of menstrual periods; can result from antipsychotic-induced high prolactin.
A mottled, net-like purplish skin pattern — a classic amantadine side effect.
Heightened skin sensitivity to sunlight causing easy sunburn — notable with chlorpromazine.
Loss of the ability to feel pleasure — a core depressive and negative symptom.
Visible slowing of movement, speech, and thought seen in depression.
Excessive sleepiness or sleeping too much — a feature of atypical depression.
Increased appetite and overeating — a feature of atypical depression.
A heavy, leaden feeling in the arms and legs — a feature of atypical depression.
A depression subtype with mood reactivity, hypersomnia, hyperphagia, leaden paralysis, and rejection sensitivity.
Worry and dread about a future event or feared situation before it happens.
A heightened, constant scanning for threat — a hyperarousal feature of PTSD.
A state of heightened alertness (startle, irritability, poor sleep) — a symptom cluster of PTSD.
A vivid, intrusive re-experiencing of a trauma as if it were happening now.
An inflated sense of self-importance, power, or ability — a hallmark of mania.
Cognitive impairment from depression that mimics dementia but improves once the mood disorder is treated.
Itching; hydroxyzine is used for its antihistamine anti-itch effect.
Describes thoughts or urges that feel alien and distressing to the person — typical of OCD obsessions.
The deficit features of schizophrenia — blunted affect, low motivation, social withdrawal, and poverty of speech.
A psychomotor syndrome of immobility, mutism, and waxy flexibility (or, at times, excitement).
A serotonergic-excess reaction: agitation, autonomic instability, and neuromuscular excitation (clonus, hyperreflexia), leg-predominant.
A rare, life-threatening reaction to dopamine blockers: fever, lead-pipe rigidity, altered consciousness, autonomic instability, and very high CK.
Drug-induced movement disorders from dopamine blockade — dystonia, akathisia, parkinsonism, and tardive dyskinesia.
The 'dry, red, hot, blind, mad' toxidrome — dry flushed skin, dilated pupils, urinary retention, hyperthermia, and delirium.
Rising lithium levels causing coarse tremor, ataxia, slurred speech, and confusion — often triggered by dehydration, NSAIDs, or thiazides.
A hypertensive emergency when someone on an MAOI eats tyramine-rich food such as aged cheese or cured meat.
Flu-like symptoms, dizziness, and 'brain zaps' after abruptly stopping a short-half-life antidepressant; prevented by tapering.
The clustering of weight gain, high blood sugar, and abnormal lipids — a major long-term risk of some antipsychotics.
Lengthening of the heart's QT interval on ECG, raising the risk of the arrhythmia torsades de pointes and sudden death.
A dangerous fall in neutrophils that leaves the body unable to fight infection — the key clozapine risk driving blood monitoring.
A low neutrophil count, increasing susceptibility to infection.
Inflammation of the heart muscle (fever, chest pain, rising troponin/CRP) that clusters in the first weeks of clozapine.
Low blood sodium; SSRIs and carbamazepine can cause it via SIADH, especially in older adults.
Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone — excess water retention producing dilutional hyponatremia.
A severe, blistering mucocutaneous drug reaction; lamotrigine and carbamazepine are classic triggers.
Drug-induced liver injury; monitor liver enzymes and stop the drug if it becomes significant.
Breakdown of muscle releasing myoglobin (very high CK) that can injure the kidneys — a feature of NMS.
Elevated prolactin from D2 blockade, causing galactorrhea, amenorrhea, and sexual dysfunction.
A dangerous diabetic emergency (high glucose with acidosis) that olanzapine or clozapine can precipitate.
Confusion from high blood ammonia — a valproate risk that can occur even with normal liver enzymes.
Bone-marrow failure to produce blood cells — a rare carbamazepine risk.
Poor iris tone during cataract surgery linked to alpha-1 blockers — flag it to ophthalmology beforehand.
Worse-than-baseline insomnia for a few nights after stopping a short-acting hypnotic.
A surge in blood pressure after abruptly stopping a central alpha-2 agonist like clonidine — always taper.
Sleep-driving, -eating, or -walking with amnesia — a boxed warning for Z-drugs like zolpidem.
Compulsive gambling, shopping, eating, or hypersexuality — a warning tied to dopamine partial agonists like aripiprazole.
A serious fetal malformation (e.g., spina bifida) linked to valproate and carbamazepine taken in pregnancy.
A congenital tricuspid-valve malformation associated with first-trimester lithium exposure.
Major depressive disorder — persistent low mood or anhedonia with sleep, appetite, energy, and cognitive changes.
An elevated or irritable mood state with grandiosity, decreased need for sleep, and risky behaviour — the manic pole of bipolar disorder.
A milder, shorter form of mania without psychosis or marked functional impairment.
A mood disorder with recurring (hypo)manic and depressive episodes, needing mood-stabilising rather than antidepressant-led treatment.
Chronic, excessive, hard-to-control worry across many areas, with restlessness, tension, and poor sleep.
Recurrent unexpected panic attacks plus persistent worry about further attacks; patients are very sensitive to early drug activation.
Intense fear of scrutiny or embarrassment in social or performance situations.
Intrusive obsessions and repetitive compulsions; often needs higher SSRI doses and a longer trial than depression.
Intrusive re-experiencing, avoidance, negative mood, and hyperarousal lasting over a month after a traumatic event.
A sustained major depressive episode arising in pregnancy or after birth — distinct from transient 'baby blues'.
A chronic psychotic disorder with positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions), negative symptoms, and cognitive impairment.
Hallucinations and delusions in established Parkinson disease, driven partly by dopaminergic therapy; avoid high-potency D2 blockers.
An acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention and awareness from an underlying medical cause; inattention is central.
A progressive, acquired decline in memory and other cognitive domains that impairs daily function.
Restless, overactive behaviour with rising hostility that can escalate to a behavioural emergency.
Problematic alcohol use with impaired control, tolerance, and a withdrawal syndrome on stopping.
Severe alcohol-withdrawal delirium — confusion, autonomic surge, and seizures; benzodiazepines are first-line and life-saving.
A thiamine-deficiency emergency: confusion, ophthalmoplegia, and ataxia; give thiamine before any glucose.
Compulsive opioid use with tolerance and withdrawal; treated with opioid agonist therapy.
Maintenance of opioid use disorder with methadone or buprenorphine to prevent withdrawal and cravings.
Nicotine dependence; cessation is aided by varenicline, bupropion, or nicotine replacement.
Persistent inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity that impairs function across settings.
Difficulty falling or staying asleep with daytime consequences; first-line treatment is CBT-I.
A chronic neurological disorder of excessive daytime sleepiness, sometimes with cataplexy.
Pain arising from nerve damage; duloxetine and TCAs are used to treat it.
A chronic widespread pain and fatigue syndrome; duloxetine is an approved treatment.
Severe, shock-like facial pain along the trigeminal nerve; carbamazepine is a classic treatment.
Involuntary night-time bed-wetting; imipramine is a recognised second-line option in children.
A prolonged or repeated seizure without recovery between events — a neurologic emergency.
A tendency to recurrent unprovoked seizures; bupropion is contraindicated because it lowers the seizure threshold.
Fear and avoidance of situations where escape might be hard or help unavailable if panic strikes.
Excessive water drinking not driven by a physiologic need, which can dilute blood sodium (and lithium) levels.
The time for a drug's blood level to fall by half; it governs dosing frequency and how long effects and withdrawal last.
Gradually adjusting a drug's dose up or down to balance benefit against tolerability.
An inactive drug the body converts into its active form (e.g., lisdexamfetamine hydrolyzed to dextroamphetamine).
A breakdown product that is itself pharmacologically active and can prolong or shape a drug's effect.
A drug-free interval when switching medications, letting the first drug clear to avoid dangerous interactions.
A drug that binds a receptor but activates it only weakly, buffering signalling up or down (e.g., aripiprazole, buprenorphine).
A drug that binds a receptor and produces the opposite effect of an agonist, lowering its baseline activity (pimavanserin at 5-HT2A).
A drug binding a site separate from the main one that amplifies the natural ligand's effect — how benzodiazepines boost GABA.
A liver conjugation step (no CYP involved) that clears drugs like lorazepam — spared in liver disease.
When a drug boosts the enzymes that metabolise it, lowering its own level over the first weeks (carbamazepine).
When a drug speeds up metabolic enzymes, lowering levels of itself or co-prescribed drugs (e.g., contraceptive failure).
A liver enzyme that metabolises many psychotropics; inhibitors like fluoxetine and bupropion raise substrate levels.
A major drug-metabolising liver enzyme; its inhibitors and inducers shift the levels of many co-prescribed drugs.
A liver enzyme inhibited by fluvoxamine and induced by cigarette smoke that clears drugs like duloxetine and clozapine.
A drug label's strongest safety warning, flagging a serious or life-threatening risk.
A long-acting injectable antipsychotic that releases drug slowly over weeks, giving reliable levels and aiding adherence.
A steroid that acts on brain GABA-A receptors; brexanolone and zuranolone are neurosteroids for postpartum depression.
A drug that depletes presynaptic dopamine (tetrabenazine, valbenazine) — the evidence-based treatment for tardive dyskinesia.
A drug that blocks the glutamate NMDA receptor (memantine, ketamine/esketamine).
A drug that raises acetylcholine by blocking its breakdown — the mainstay for mild-to-moderate Alzheimer (donepezil, rivastigmine).
Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor — the first-line class of antidepressants (e.g., sertraline, escitalopram).
Serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor antidepressant (e.g., venlafaxine, duloxetine), also useful for pain.
Monoamine oxidase inhibitor — a potent, reserved antidepressant needing a low-tyramine diet and drug washout intervals.
Reversible inhibitor of MAO-A (moclobemide) — dietary tyramine displaces it, so no strict diet is needed and washout is short.
Tricyclic antidepressant — effective but anticholinergic, sedating, and dangerous (cardiotoxic) in overdose.
A four-ringed antidepressant such as maprotiline or mirtazapine.
A GABA-A positive allosteric modulator used for anxiety, sedation, and seizures; tolerance and dependence limit long-term use.
Non-benzodiazepine hypnotics (zopiclone, zolpidem) that act at the same GABA-A site for sleep.
A newer ('atypical') antipsychotic — generally lower EPS but more metabolic risk than older agents.
An older ('typical') antipsychotic — a potent D2 blocker with more extrapyramidal side effects.
A hypnotic that blocks wake-promoting orexin (a DORA), covering both sleep onset and maintenance.
Norepinephrine-dopamine reuptake inhibitor (bupropion) — no serotonergic activity, so no sexual dysfunction or weight gain.
A central alpha-2 adrenergic agonist (clonidine, guanfacine) causing sedation, low blood pressure, and a slow pulse.
An alpha-1 adrenergic blocker (prazosin) that lowers blood pressure and eases PTSD nightmares; watch for first-dose hypotension.
A gabapentinoid (gabapentin, pregabalin) binding the alpha-2-delta calcium-channel subunit for anxiety and neuropathic pain.
A characteristic cluster of signs pointing to a class of poisoning (e.g., anticholinergic, sympathomimetic, opioid).
Producing adrenaline-like effects — agitation, tachycardia, hypertension, dilated pupils, and hyperthermia.
The brain's resistance to seizing; drugs like bupropion lower it, raising seizure risk in a dose-dependent way.
Needing more drug over time for the same effect; it is lost after a break, so agents like clozapine are restarted low.
A physiological adaptation to a drug such that stopping or reducing it triggers a withdrawal syndrome.
Neuronal injury from excessive glutamate signalling — the process memantine's NMDA blockade is meant to limit.
Abrupt, severe withdrawal triggered by giving an antagonist or partial agonist (buprenorphine, naloxone) to an opioid-dependent person.
Return of opioid overdose when short-acting naloxone wears off before the longer-acting opioid does.
A drug level drawn at its lowest point just before the next dose — how lithium and similar agents are monitored.
Liver function tests — blood markers (ALT, AST) followed to detect drug-induced liver injury.
Complete blood count — screens for agranulocytosis, neutropenia, and anemia.
Absolute neutrophil count — the number tracked on clozapine to catch agranulocytosis early.
Creatine kinase — a muscle enzyme that rises markedly in NMS and rhabdomyolysis.
A blood marker of heart-muscle injury; it rises in clozapine-induced myocarditis.
C-reactive protein — an inflammation marker that rises alongside troponin in myocarditis.
Abnormal Involuntary Movement Scale — a standardised exam to screen for and track tardive dyskinesia.
A genetic allele (common in some Asian ancestries) screened before carbamazepine because it raises Stevens-Johnson risk.
Clinical Opiate Withdrawal Scale — scores withdrawal severity to time the start of buprenorphine.
